facts.text: Ralph Samuelson is widely credited as the inventor of water skiing, first successfully skiing on Lake Pepin, Minnesota, on July 2, 1922.
Slalom water skiing uses a single ski with two foot bindings, requiring the skier to weave through a course of six buoys.
MasterCraft, founded in 1968, is one of the most iconic tournament water ski boat manufacturers in the world.
Lake Havasu, straddling the Arizona-California border, is one of the most popular water skiing destinations in the United States.
A standard water ski tow rope is 75 feet (23 meters) long for slalom skiing.
Trick skiing involves performing spins, flips, and other acrobatic maneuvers on the water surface within two 20-second passes.
The International Waterski & Wakeboard Federation (IWWF) is the global governing body for water skiing and wakeboarding.
Barefoot water skiing requires speeds of approximately 35-45 mph, significantly faster than conventional water skiing.
Cypress Gardens in Winter Haven, Florida, was known as the 'Water Ski Capital of the World' and hosted spectacular water ski shows from 1943 to 2009.
A proper deep water start in water skiing involves keeping your knees bent, arms straight, and letting the boat pull you out of the water.
Lake Powell, on the Utah-Arizona border, offers over 1,900 miles of shoreline and is a premier water skiing destination.
Parks Bonifay became the youngest person to complete a wakeboarding inverted trick at just 9 years old.
Water ski boats are specifically designed to create minimal wake for slalom skiing, unlike wakeboard boats that create large wakes.
The Masters Water Ski & Wakeboard Tournament, held at Callaway Gardens in Pine Mountain, Georgia, is one of the most prestigious events in the sport.
Andy Mapple, a British slalom skier, won 13 consecutive Masters titles and is considered one of the greatest slalom skiers of all time.
Water skiing hand signals are essential for communication between the skier and the boat driver, with a thumb up meaning 'speed up' and thumb down meaning 'slow down.'
Lake Tahoe, sitting at 6,225 feet elevation on the California-Nevada border, offers stunning alpine water skiing with crystal-clear visibility.
Combo skis, also called trainer skis, are a pair of wider skis ideal for beginners learning to water ski.
Show skiing combines water skiing with theatrical performances, including human pyramids, ballet lines, and jump acts.
The American Water Ski Association (now USA Water Ski & Wake Sports) was founded in 1939.
Nate Smith holds multiple slalom water skiing world records and is widely regarded as one of the best modern slalom skiers.
Wakeboarding evolved from a combination of water skiing, snowboarding, and surfing in the late 1980s.
Nautique boats, manufactured by Correct Craft since 1925, are a top choice for professional water ski tournaments.
Lake of the Ozarks in Missouri has over 1,150 miles of shoreline and is a major water skiing hub in the Midwest.
A water ski jump ramp is typically 5 to 6 feet high for men and 5 feet for women in tournament competition.
Ralph Samuelson used two boards as skis and a clothesline as a tow rope during his first water skiing experiments in 1922.
Wakesurfing differs from wakeboarding in that the rider eventually lets go of the rope and surfs the boat's wake freely.
Perfect Pass is an electronic speed control system used in tournament ski boats to maintain a precise, consistent speed for the skier.
Regina Jaquess has won more overall water ski world championship titles than any other female skier in history.
The slalom course consists of an entry gate, six turn buoys alternating on each side, and an exit gate, all within a 259-meter course.
Flathead Lake in Montana is the largest natural freshwater lake west of the Mississippi and is famous for its clear water, perfect for skiing.
Cable parks allow wakeboarders to ride without a boat, using an overhead cable system to tow riders around a lake.
Ballast systems in wakeboard boats use tanks that fill with water to increase the boat's weight and create larger wakes.
The first National Water Ski Championships were held in 1939 at Jones Beach State Park in New York.
Freddy Krueger (the water skier, not the horror character) is a multiple-time world champion trick skier from the United States.
Tower speakers mounted on wakeboard boat towers allow riders to hear music clearly while being towed behind the boat.
Lake Geneva in Switzerland is one of Europe's most prestigious water skiing locations, surrounded by the Alps.
A dock start in water skiing involves starting from a seated position on a dock rather than from the water, requiring precise timing.
Water skiing requires a spotter in most U.S. states — a person in the boat who watches the skier at all times in addition to the driver.
Shaun Murray is a pioneering professional wakeboarder who helped popularize the sport in the 1990s and early 2000s.
Malibu Boats has been a leading manufacturer of water ski and wakeboard boats since 1982.
The world record for water ski jumping exceeds 250 feet (76 meters) for men.
Lake Como in northern Italy is a popular European destination for water skiing, known for its deep waters and scenic beauty.
In trick skiing, each trick is assigned a point value, and skiers try to accumulate the highest total score in two passes.
USA Water Ski & Wake Sports is the national governing body for water skiing, wakeboarding, and related towed water sports in the United States.
A slalom ski typically has a single deep fin on the bottom to aid in turning and edge control through the course.
Water ski shows at SeaWorld parks featured elaborate performances with ski jumps, pyramids, and choreographed routines for decades.
Table Rock Lake in Missouri is known for its clean, clear water and is a top destination for water skiing in the Ozarks.
The Malibu Open is one of professional water skiing's most important annual competitions.
Wakeboard cable parks have grown rapidly worldwide, with over 200 parks operating across Europe alone.
Water ski bindings, also called boots, come in adjustable rear toe plates and fixed front boots for slalom skiing.
The first water ski show at Cypress Gardens took place in 1943, featuring just a few skiers performing basic stunts.
Barefoot water skiing was pioneered in the 1940s and has grown into a competitive discipline with its own world championships.
In competitive slalom, the boat speed starts at 25 mph for women and 28 mph for men, increasing to a maximum of 34 and 36 mph respectively.
Lake Pepin, where water skiing was invented, is actually a naturally occurring wide section of the Mississippi River in Minnesota.
Wakeboard tricks are categorized into surface tricks, inverts (flips), spins, and grab variations.
MasterCraft's ProStar is considered one of the finest tournament slalom boats ever manufactured.
Water skiing laws vary by state, but most require the activity to take place between sunrise and sunset.
The first World Water Ski Championships were held in 1949 in Juan-les-Pins, France.
A standard wakeboard is typically between 130 and 147 centimeters long, depending on the rider's weight and skill level.
Show skiing teams often include swivel skiing, where the skier spins continuously on the water while being towed.
Deep freshwater lakes are ideal for water skiing because they tend to have calmer, glassier water surfaces.
The 'OK' hand signal from a skier in the water tells the boat driver that the skier is fine after a fall.
Trick ski ropes are shorter than slalom ropes, typically around 45 to 55 feet, to keep the skier closer to the boat's wake.
Lake Havasu City hosts numerous water sports tournaments throughout the year, including professional water ski events.
Andy Mapple moved from England to the United States to train on American lakes and dominate the slalom circuit.
Wakesurfing boats must create a large, clean wave, which is achieved through specialized hull designs and ballast tank placement.
The three events in traditional competitive water skiing are slalom, tricks, and jumping.
Ski Nautique boats have been the official towboat for the Masters tournament for decades.
A water skier's life jacket must be U.S. Coast Guard approved and properly fitted to be legal in most states.
The Raley is one of wakeboarding's foundational tricks, involving an edge-to-edge motion that extends the body behind the rider.
Ralph Samuelson also performed the first water ski jump in 1925, using a greased ramp.
Lake of the Ozarks' mile marker system helps boaters navigate its winding 92-mile main channel.
In water ski jumping, men use a 6-foot ramp and women use a 5-foot ramp at maximum tournament speed.
Knee boarding is a related towed water sport where the rider kneels on a small board rather than standing.
The Tantrum is a popular wakeboarding trick that involves a backflip performed off the wake.
Correct Craft, makers of Nautique boats, is one of the oldest boat manufacturers in the U.S., founded in 1925.
Water skiing at altitude, such as on Lake Tahoe, requires adjustments because the thinner air affects boat engine performance.
The slalom course rope is shortened progressively in competition, making each pass more difficult as the skier must reach wider to round the buoys.
Flathead Lake is so clear that objects can be seen up to 35 feet below the surface in some areas.
Cable wakeboarding features unique obstacles like kickers, rails, and fun boxes placed around the lake.
Trick skis are shorter, wider, and have no fins, allowing the skier to spin and slide freely on the water.
The first professional water ski tour was established in the 1970s, bringing competitive skiing to a wider audience.
Wakeboard towers are A-frame or T-frame structures mounted on boats that elevate the tow point for better air on jumps.
Water skiing was demonstrated as an exhibition sport at the 1972 Munich Olympics.
In barefoot skiing, the three events are slalom (crossing the wakes), tricks, and jumping.
Lake Geneva's annual water ski shows have been a staple of summer entertainment in Wisconsin since the 1960s.
The whip turn in slalom skiing is the technique of carving aggressively through the wake to build speed toward the buoy.
Ski boats produce a very small, flat wake, while wakeboard boats are designed to produce a large, steep wake.
Nate Smith set his first slalom world record at the age of 28, running the course at record-short line lengths.
Water skiing tow boats typically range from 19 to 24 feet in length for tournament use.
The Tommy Bartlett Water Show in Wisconsin Dells ran for over 50 years, featuring world-class water ski performances.
Table Rock Lake was created by the completion of Table Rock Dam on the White River in 1958.
Wakesurfing has grown more popular than wakeboarding in recent years, particularly among recreational boaters.
In show skiing, the human pyramid can stack up to five tiers of skiers, with the entire formation pulled by a single boat.
The IWWF World Water Ski Championships are held every two years in different countries around the world.
A typical tournament water ski boat weighs between 3,000 and 5,000 pounds, creating a predictable and consistent wake.
Water skiing became an official Pan American Games sport in 1995.
The KD Skis brand, founded by Herb O'Brien, pioneered the use of fiberglass in water ski construction in the 1960s.
Wakeboarders typically ride at speeds between 18 and 24 mph, slower than water skiers who ride at 26 to 36 mph.
Lake Powell's red rock canyons and blue waters create one of the most scenic water skiing environments in the world.
The claw hand signal, made by a downed skier, indicates to other boats that there is a skier in the water.
Parks Bonifay was inducted into the Wakeboarding Hall of Fame and is known for inventing numerous tricks.
Slalom competition line lengths are measured from the pylon on the boat, starting at 75 feet and shortening to as little as 9.75 meters.
Water skiing burns approximately 350 to 500 calories per hour, depending on intensity.
The first motorboat used for water skiing was a simple outboard motor vessel on Lake Pepin in 1922.
Wakeboard bindings are similar to snowboard bindings and come in open-toe and closed-toe designs.
Lake Como reaches a maximum depth of 1,345 feet, making it one of the deepest lakes in Europe.
The toe hold in barefoot skiing allows the skier to perform tricks using a toe strap attached to the tow rope handle.
Water ski competitions are judged by a panel of officials using strict IWWF or USA Water Ski rules.
MasterCraft's X-Star is a popular crossover boat designed for both water skiing and wakeboarding.
Cypress Gardens' water ski shows inspired the creation of water ski attractions at theme parks worldwide.
In trick skiing, surface tricks like wake turns, 180s, and 360s are scored lower than wake tricks performed in the air.
Water skiing is practiced in over 80 countries worldwide, making it one of the most popular water sports globally.
The Malibu Response is a dedicated tournament slalom boat known for its exceptionally flat wake.
Freddy Krueger has accumulated more trick skiing world records than any other male skier.
Lake Havasu's London Bridge, relocated from England in 1971, serves as a landmark for water skiers on the lake.

Lake Geneva, Wisconsin (not Switzerland), is also a popular water skiing destination in the American Midwest.
